Revenue will be about $11.3 billion in the fiscal second quarter, which ends in July, Salesforce said Wednesday in a statement. Analysts, on average, projected $11.4 billion. The company also reported fiscal first-quarter remaining performance obligations — a measure of future sales — were $67.9 billion, compared with analysts’ average estimate of $68.9 billion. 

The company's AI offering, Agentforce, is now on track to contribute $1.2 billion of annual revenue, up from $800 million in February. Use of AI models within Salesforce’s platform has more than doubled compared to the prior quarter, the company said. Salesforce continues to expect revenue growth to accelerate in the second half of the year.

$SNOW Snowflake, the maker of software that helps organize, analyze and store corporate data in the cloud is seeing shares surging 30% and into the green YTD after a stronger-than-expected annual outlook for product sales.

Rather than being "eaten by AI," the firm is using AI to build new tools. Customers using the company’s AI-assisted coding tool doubled from the prior quarter, reaching 7,100, Chief Executive Officer Sridhar Ramaswamy said in an interview.

The company is seeing increased demand for its main data products at the same time that its newer AI-focused tools have become “legitimate businesses in their own right,” Ramaswamy said.

Product revenue will increase about 31% to $5.84 billion in the fiscal year ending in January 2027, Snowflake said Wednesday in a statement. That’s an increase from a previous outlook of $5.66 billion given in February and topped analysts’ average estimate of $5.68 billion. Remaining performance obligations, a measure of bookings, though were $9.21 billion, falling short of the average analyst estimate of $9.43 billion.

Separately, Snowflake made a new commitment to spend an additional $6 billion on https://t.co/pRHcRieAZr Inc.’s Web Services. This includes use of Amazon’s general-purpose Graviton processor chips that compete with Intel Corp.’s offerings. “Our teams work exceptionally well together and we drive a lot of joint business,” Ramaswamy said of the agreement.

US home foreclosures are accelerating:

Properties with foreclosure filings rose +26% YoY in Q1 2026, to ~119,000, the highest in 6 years.

The last time foreclosures were at this high was Q1 2020, just before government relief programs and pandemic stimulus caused them to decline.

Foreclosure filings have more than TRIPLED since the post-pandemic low.

The increase has been driven by soaring home insurance bills, property taxes, and homeowner association fees rather than mortgage defaults alone.

In 2025, the average annual home insurance bill jumped +12% YoY, to a record $2,948.

At the same time, average property tax on US single-family homes rose +3% YoY, to $4,427, also the highest on record.

Financial pressures on homeowners are surging.

BREAKING: Home prices across America's 20 largest cities fell -0.16% MoM in March, the 2nd-consecutive monthly decline after 6-straight monthly increases.

YoY, prices rose +0.83%, the weakest annual gain since July 2023.

This comes as over half of the 20 major US housing markets posted YoY price decreases.

Seattle posted the steepest decline at -2.5%, followed by Denver at -2.0%, Tampa at -1.9%, Dallas at -1.7%, and Los Angeles and Phoenix at -1.6%.

Meanwhile, the spread between the strongest and weakest markets stands at 8.6 percentage points, highlighting how uneven the housing slowdown has become.

In real terms, home prices fell for their 10th consecutive month, as inflation continued to outpace nominal price gains.

The US housing market is cooling.

Crypto cards are the ultimate use case for stablecoins.

Japan's oil market is experiencing a historic shock:

Japan's crude oil imports dropped -59% MoM, to 900,000 barrels per day in April, following the outbreak of the Iran War and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

This comes as Japan relies on the Middle East for over 90% of its crude oil supply.

Meanwhile, May imports are projected to rise +89% MoM, to 1.7 million barrels per day, or ~75% of pre-war levels.

This recovery is expected to be supported by alternative crude procurement from outside the Strait of Hormuz, including US supplies and Saudi Arabian cargoes via the Red Sea, as well as shipments that transited the Strait in late April.

The country has also conducted two emergency oil reserve releases to stabilize its market, with stockpiles now down to 202 days of supply, from 243 days at the end of February.

Japan is scrambling for oil alternatives.

tweets.text:
Avoiding Death on the Yellow Brick Road: Why The App Layer Isn't Dead

The Yellow Brick Road is our shorthand for the path the labs are walking, where they’re committing extraordinary resources.

The reason the labs are best-suited for problems like code generation, writing, or image-creation is because these problems improve with raw model capability: every dollar spent on pre-training and post-training improves product quality.

Meanwhile, the rest of Oz is inhabited by more complex, often vertical problems, that aren’t as simple as giving a business user a horizontal tool with access to standard tools and computer use.

The value comes less from the underlying model’s raw capability (though that’s still important!) than from the scaffolding around it that makes the output trustworthy, compliant, and operational inside a specific industry.

Full piece by @joeschmidtiv: https://t.co/84QN5Mj9T3

tweets.text:
🌎 Hier soir, j’ai eu le plaisir d’intervenir au Monaco Economic Board (@MEB_Monaco) pour partager notre lecture de l’environnement macroéconomique et géopolitique actuel. L’objectif de cette intervention était d’essayer de comprendre les forces à l’œuvre, identifier les risques, mais aussi les zones de résilience.

🇨🇳 Nous avons d’abord évoqué la Chine, dont la croissance reste solide en apparence, mais repose sur des fondations plus fragiles : soutien public, faiblesse de la demande domestique, crise immobilière persistante, pression sur la confiance des ménages et volonté assumée de Pékin d’accélérer sur la souveraineté technologique. Derrière les chiffres de croissance, l’enjeu est clair : la Chine veut moins dépendre du reste du monde, renforcer ses capacités industrielles stratégiques et relancer sa consommation intérieure.

🇺🇸 Nous avons ensuite abordé les États-Unis, où le scénario de récession est à exclure, malgré des risques qui se multiplient. La croissance américaine reste soutenue par plusieurs forces : impulsion budgétaire, effet richesse des ménages, investissements dans l'intelligence artificielle et gains de productivité. Mais cette résilience a un prix : déficits élevés, tensions sur les taux longs, incertitude politique et inflation encore trop résistante pour permettre un vrai retour à la normalité monétaire.

🇪🇺 Enfin, nous avons parlé de la zone euro, probablement la zone la plus délicate à analyser aujourd’hui. Le risque n’est pas seulement celui d’un ralentissement, mais celui d’un environnement plus inconfortable : croissance faible, inflation qui peut repartir sous l’effet de l’énergie et marges de manœuvre budgétaires limitées. Autrement dit, un risque de stagflation qui oblige la BCE à arbitrer entre soutien à l’activité et crédibilité sur les prix.

Le message central de cette conférence était que nous ne sommes pas simplement dans un cycle économique classique mais dans une phase de transition où l’économie, la géopolitique, l’énergie, la technologie et les politiques publiques sont de plus en plus imbriquées. Pour les investisseurs, les entreprises et les décideurs, cela impose de raisonner davantage en scénarios qu’en certitudes.

Un grand merci au Monaco Economic Board pour l’organisation de cet événement, à EFG Bank Monaco pour son précieux soutien, ainsi qu’à l’ensemble des participants pour la qualité des échanges et des questions.

Très heureux d’avoir pu contribuer à ce moment de réflexion dans un cadre aussi stimulant.

#Macroeconomics #Geopolitics #Markets #Monaco #EconomicOutlook #Investment #China #UnitedStates #Eurozone

Large IPOs in a sector do not automatically mean “excess supply.”

They often signal the opposite:

>Investors want more exposure

>Relative valuations remain attractive

>The public market still lacks enough pure-play vehicles for the theme

The world creates new savings and investment capital every day. Expanding equity supply is normal in a growing economy.

Don’t be the midwit fading every new deal.

tweets.text:
Our regulatory framework is built around companies, and that doesn't fit what blockchains actually are. @milesjennings on the mismatch:

"Our entire regulatory framework from securities laws to corporate law, everything basically assumes that there is a company at the middle that controls and that control persists over time. When you apply that to building networks, right, what it means is that those networks end up being controlled by a centralized company."

"An incredible amount of power accrues to that company at the middle rather than to the people that are actually using that network and the ones that make it valuable."

"Blockchains allow you to remove the company from that and have a network that is fully autonomous on its own. And as a result of that, no one controls it."

@LawofCodeFM

tweets.text:
Agents are getting very good at finding DeFi bugs. That’s exactly why defenders should be using them too.

Security is asymmetric, but AI cuts both ways: use it to find and eradicate bugs before attackers do.

As @daejunpark and @mg_486662 wrote, agents are already useful for identifying vulnerabilities and reducing the burden of manual review.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
PSA: I now consider *all* of DeFi unsafe.

Coding agents are superhuman at finding vulnerabilities, and smart contract security is too asymmetric: defenders need to fix every bug while attackers need just one exploit to steal funds.

tweets.text:
Countertrend rallies in bear markets often bring out a lot of emotion.

People become anchored to short-term price movements and forget that volatility exists in both directions.

I have no interest in personal attacks or tribalism. My goal has always been to approach markets probabilistically, manage risk, and adapt as new data comes in.

Time will determine which framework best described the cycle.

That would make labour even more expensive, squeeze workers’ purchasing power and weigh further on growth and prosperity.
